summary: Jeff Atwood explores the common practice of 'dogfooding' (using your own software product) and argues that while it's clearly beneficial, it's harder to implement effectively than often acknowledged. He identifies three key obstacles: developers aren't representative of typical users, specialized business applications require domain knowledge developers lack, and working with low-quality software you can't control is demoralizing. Despite these challenges, he still advocates for aggressive dogfooding whenever possible, and suggests working the help desk as an alternative way to understand user pain when direct dogfooding isn't feasible.
key_points: Dogfooding works well when developers are the target audience (like Visual Studio or operating systems), but developers are highly unrepresentative of typical users and may add overly complex features
Specialized business applications require domain expertise that developers often lack, making meaningful dogfooding nearly impossible without significant exposure to the target audience
The quality and direction of team projects is often outside individual developers' control, making it psychologically difficult to use software you're not proud of
When direct dogfooding isn't possible, creating dedicated dogfooding test plans with real customers or working the help desk can provide similar user perspective benefits
money_quotes: Trying to use the product, as a customer would, found these showstoppers in a minute.
If the application you're writing isn't intended for expert users, having the developers dogfood it won't necessarily buy you much, because developers are highly unrepresentative of typical users.
Beyond fixing critical bugs, it could even hurt the application: developers tend to add advanced, complicated features that are useful to them.
The only dogfood guaranteed to taste good is your own.

If you are developing a product that you can't integrate into your day to day job then you should be creating a dogfooding test plan where you and interested customers spend dedicated time using the product the way it is meant to be used.
There's nothing quite as effective as sharing the customer's pain.
key_insight: Dogfooding is a valuable practice, but its effectiveness depends heavily on whether developers actually resemble the target users—and when they don't, teams need alternative strategies like dedicated test plans or help desk rotations to gain genuine user perspective.
